narrative: Dear Maria, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, and unfortunately, the picture that emerged made them worry about your chances of passing the bar exam. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile at school—that was the biggest red flag, whispering doubts about whether you'd mastered the toughest material when it really counted. Even your Year 1 decile of 5, right in the middle, didn't inspire full confidence, suggesting your performance hadn't climbed as strongly as they'd hoped. Your LSAT score of 29.5 added to those concerns, falling short of the benchmarks that predict bar success for others. Coming from an upper-middle family income, the model saw some resources there but still leaned toward risk, perhaps because it hasn't always shielded students from these academic hurdles. Your Hispanic background played a role too, reflecting patterns where systemic challenges have tipped the scales against passing. With a solid but not standout undergrad GPA of 3.1, and planning to go full-time without work distractions, those factors nudged the prediction further toward a 47% chance of failure. Being a woman didn't shift things much in a positive direction here either. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they all wove together into a cautious tale. This isn't the end of your story; many have rewritten theirs by targeting those Year 3 grades or LSAT prep. You've got the drive—your target law passion shines through. Keep pushing, and the next chapter could surprise everyone.
